retrieved_docs.title: Anatomy, Head and Neck, Tooth Eruption -- Clinical Significance -- Ectopic Eruption
retrieved_docs.content: An ectopic eruption is a tooth following a path that deviates from the norm. [17] Timely diagnosis and management of ectopic teeth are essential to prevent future malocclusion. Various factors, including ethnicity, gender, and individual traits, influence the variation in tooth position. A localized traumatic injury can displace 1 or more tooth buds, resulting in an ectopic eruption of the affected teeth. Other local factors contributing to ectopic eruptions include the presence of fibrous tissue, supernumerary teeth, arch length deficiency, and the retention or premature loss of deciduous teeth.

retrieved_docs.title: Anatomy, Head and Neck, Tooth Eruption -- Structure and Function -- Eruption Patterns
retrieved_docs.content: The eruption timing of deciduous and permanent teeth can exhibit significant variation. The eruption sequence holds greater significance than the timing, as it may vary between deciduous and permanent teeth. Usually, a variation of up to 6 months before or after the typical eruption date is considered within the normal range.

retrieved_docs.title: Anatomy, Permanent Dentition -- Physiologic Variants
retrieved_docs.content: Hyperdontia is a condition characterized by additional teeth within the normal dentition, termed supernumerary teeth. Supernumerary teeth are frequently located in the anterior maxilla, between the central incisors, termed mesiodens. [33] In permanent dentition, the prevalence of supernumerary teeth ranges between 0.5% to 5.3%. [34] [35] It affects males more frequently than females, with a ratio of 2 to 1. This phenomenon is often associated with syndromes such as Ehlers-Danlos, cleidocranial dysplasia, and Gardner syndrome. However, it may also be seen in non-syndromic patients. It is also more commonly seen in males than females. As a result of their formation in any part of the dental arch, this can lead to complications, including delayed eruption, malposition, impaction, diastemas, crowding, and poor aesthetics. [33] [34] [35] True anodontia is the absence of primary teeth, and total anodontia is complete tooth agenesis in both the primary and permanent dentition, both of which are extremely rare. [36]

retrieved_docs.title: Developmental Disturbances of the Teeth, Anomalies of Structure -- Differential Diagnosis -- Dens Evaginatus/Talon Cusp
retrieved_docs.content: Gemination Fusion Enamel hyperplasia

retrieved_docs.title: Anatomy, Head and Neck, Tooth Eruption -- Clinical Significance
retrieved_docs.content: Tooth eruption is an intricate and meticulously regulated process that influences the healthy development of the craniofacial region. Factors such as eruption timing, sequence, direction, rate, position, and morphology of teeth significantly contribute to facial aesthetics and phonetics. [13] Predicting the eruption schedule is a valuable tool for strategizing a child's dental care, which includes diagnostic, preventive, and therapeutic measures. [14]

retrieved_docs.title: Anatomy, Permanent Dentition -- Physiologic Variants
retrieved_docs.content: Dilaceration describes an unusually abrupt angle between tooth crown and root or within the root itself, resulting from trauma during the development of the tooth. Further anomalies of the dental hard tissues include gemination, where a single tooth forms two crowns. This is a consequence of the tooth bud's partial division, resulting in a bifid crown but retaining the normal number of expected teeth within the dental arch. [30] However, fusion leads to one less tooth within the dental arch as it comprises two separate developing tooth germs which join together. [31] Concrescence is a rare phenomenon where adjacent teeth are united by cementum. [32]

retrieved_docs.title: Anatomy, Head and Neck, Tooth Eruption -- Clinical Significance -- Delayed Eruption
retrieved_docs.content: When the timing of tooth emergence significantly deviates from the established norms associated with ethnicity and gender, it is referred to as delayed eruption. [2] Delayed eruption can result from various causes, including local factors such as mucosal barriers, gingival fibromatosis, odontogenic and nonodontogenic tumors, premature loss of a primary tooth, and radiation damage. Moreover, delayed eruption can be associated with systemic conditions and endocrine disorders such as hypothyroidism, [19] hypopituitarism, hypoparathyroidism, and certain medications. [2]

retrieved_docs.title: Developmental Disturbances of the Teeth, Anomalies of Structure -- Differential Diagnosis -- Dens Invaginatus
retrieved_docs.content: Fusion Enamel hypoplasia Gemination

retrieved_docs.title: Anatomy, Head and Neck, Tooth Eruption -- Clinical Significance -- Delayed Eruption
retrieved_docs.content: Chronic and prolonged malnutrition, often associated with vitamin deficiencies during childhood, is another factor that also correlates with delayed eruption. [2] Moreover, various syndromes have been identified as causing delayed eruption, including Down syndrome, Gardner syndrome, cleidocranial dysostosis, anhidrotic ectodermal dysplasia, Hutchinson-Gilford syndrome, Bloch–Sulzberger syndrome, Apert syndrome, and Axenfeld–Rieger syndrome. [2]

retrieved_docs.title: Developmental Disturbances of the Teeth, Anomalies of Number -- Differential Diagnosis -- Hyperdontia
retrieved_docs.content: For patients with hyperdontia, the following differential diagnoses should be considered: Genetic syndromes, eg, cleidocranial dysplasia, Gardener syndrome, or trichorhinophalangeal syndrome Variation of normal anatomy

retrieved_docs.title: Developmental Disturbances of the Teeth, Anomalies of Shape and Size -- Differential Diagnosis
retrieved_docs.content: The differential diagnosis for each anomalous condition is outlined below. Additional diagnostic tools, such as radiographic evaluation and genetic testing, may aid in distinguishing between these conditions.

retrieved_docs.title: Developmental Disturbances of the Teeth, Anomalies of Structure -- Introduction
retrieved_docs.content: Dental development is a highly regulated biological process and results from several specific and highly regulated sequences of steps. Teeth are comprised of many different internal and external structures with a variety of developmental and embryologic origins. If any of these developmental processes are disturbed or over-exaggerated, changes can occur within the developed tooth’s structure. These changes can be as simple as a slight overexpression of a singular tooth structure to the complete obliteration of dental tissue in final development. The developmental anomalies to be discussed in this article are as follows: dens evaginatus, dens invaginatus, amelogenesis imperfecta, and dentinogenesis imperfecta.

retrieved_docs.title: Developmental Disturbances of the Teeth, Anomalies of Structure -- Differential Diagnosis -- Dentinogenesis Imperfecta
retrieved_docs.content: Dentin dysplasia Osteogenesis imperfecta Amelogenesis imperfecta

retrieved_docs.title: Developmental Disturbances of the Teeth, Anomalies of Number -- Histopathology
retrieved_docs.content: In regions where 1 or more teeth are absent, microscopic examination typically reveals bone levels that range from normal to slightly reduced. These changes reflect the lack of mechanical stimulation and natural development that occurs when teeth are missing.

retrieved_docs.title: Anatomy, Head and Neck, Tooth Eruption -- Clinical Significance
retrieved_docs.content: Disruptions in tooth eruption present frequent challenges in dental practice, manifesting as delays, complete impairment, premature eruption, or teeth emerging in ectopic positions. A summary of the prevalent complications associated with tooth eruption is provided below.

retrieved_docs.title: Developmental Disturbances of the Teeth, Anomalies of Shape and Size -- Differential Diagnosis -- Microdontia
retrieved_docs.content: Pituitary dwarfism Hypopituitarism Growth hormone-related defects

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 20 -- Level 3 -- Community 2106
retrieved_docs.content: فالج is a general medical condition, and فالج طفلي represents a more specific, narrower form of فالج, indicating the infantile variant of this condition.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 30 -- Level 4 -- Community 2732
retrieved_docs.content: Other specified conditions related to pregnancy comprise a range of distinct gestational disorders. This category includes renal disorder during pregnancy, peripheral neuropathy during pregnancy, and polymorphic eruption of pregnancy, each representing a separate organ‑system complication that can arise in the context of gestation and is classified under the broader label of other specified pregnancy‑related conditions.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 1 -- Community 243
retrieved_docs.content: Disorders of teeth and jaw are classified under the broader parent category zuby and are specifically associated with the anatomical location of zuby mléčné, the primary (milk) teeth.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 1 -- Community 72
retrieved_docs.content: The category “diseases of mouth; excluding dental” is classified as a subcategory of “اِضطِراب الغدة اللُّعابِيَّة” (salivary gland disorder), indicating that non‑dental oral diseases are considered part of the broader spectrum of salivary gland pathology. This relationship highlights the importance of evaluating salivary gland function when assessing oral conditions that are not of dental origin, thereby guiding more comprehensive diagnosis and patient care.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 2 -- Level 1 -- Community 249
retrieved_docs.content: Dental procedures are performed directly on zuby mléčné (the primary or “milk” teeth), indicating that therapeutic dental interventions often target the primary dentition. Likewise, disorders of teeth and jaw are located in zuby mléčné, showing that many oral pathologies also affect these early‑life teeth. Together, these relationships emphasize that both clinical treatments and disease processes in pediatric dentistry are closely linked to the primary teeth, underscoring the importance of careful assessment and management of zuby mléčné in patient care.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 1 -- Level 1 -- Community 225
retrieved_docs.content: Extraction of single tooth is a direct procedure performed on zuby (teeth), indicating that the teeth are the specific anatomical site for this dental intervention. Disorders of teeth and jaw are described as having zuby as their parent category, meaning that conditions affecting the teeth and jaw are organized under the broader classification of zuby. Consequently, zuby functions both as the procedural target for single‑tooth extraction and as the foundational element within the hierarchy of dental and jaw disorders, underscoring its central importance in dental treatment and pathology.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 1 -- Level 2 -- Community 362
retrieved_docs.content: Other bone disease and musculoskeletal deformities are associated with bone resorption, indicating that these conditions often involve increased breakdown of bone tissue, which contributes to skeletal weakening and structural abnormalities.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 9 -- Level 1 -- Community 93
retrieved_docs.content: abnormalities, treatment‑related complications, and a wide array of multilingual disease entities into a comprehensive classification system that supports accurate diagnosis, coding, reporting, and patient management across diverse medical domains.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 4 -- Level 3 -- Community 2732
retrieved_docs.content: Accidental physical contact (event) is described as being due to loss of teeth caused by an accident, indicating that dental injury resulting from an accident can lead to unintended physical contact.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 6 -- Level 3 -- Community 1494
retrieved_docs.content: A gestational age of 29‑30 weeks completed is associated with a liveborn infant, indicating that pregnancies reaching this stage can result in a live birth.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 6 -- Level 3 -- Community 2004
retrieved_docs.content: The term “Abnormal” is used as an interpretation of فقر دم فانكوني (Fanconi anemia), indicating that the clinical and laboratory findings associated with this genetic disorder are considered abnormal. Fanconi anemia is a hereditary condition characterized by bone‑marrow failure, congenital anomalies, and a heightened risk of malignancies, and the descriptor “Abnormal” reflects the pathological nature of its presentations.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 2 -- Level 1 -- Community 250
retrieved_docs.content: The extraction of a single tooth is a direct dental procedure performed on the zuby (teeth), involving the removal of one tooth from the oral cavity.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 3 -- Community 2181
retrieved_docs.content: Metastatic malignant neoplasm to sublingual gland is classified as a malignant tumor in the sublingual gland (ورم خبيث في الغدة تحت اللسان), indicating that this metastatic cancer represents a specific form of malignant neoplasm affecting the sublingual salivary gland. Recognizing this relationship clarifies the pathological nature and precise anatomical location of the disease, which is essential for accurate diagnosis, staging, and therapeutic planning in patients with salivary gland malignancies.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 13 -- Level 2 -- Community 560
retrieved_docs.content: targeted screenings, acute and chronic disease subtypes, genetic and toxicologic conditions, laboratory abnormalities, malignancies, trauma, and specialized organ disorders within a unified, multi‑lingual hierarchical structure that facilitates accurate coding, comprehensive patient assessment, and coordinated care across clinical and administrative settings.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 1 -- Level 1 -- Community 226
retrieved_docs.content: Dental procedures are directly performed on zuby mléčné, indicating that primary (baby) teeth serve as the specific sites for these dental interventions.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 1 -- Community 244
retrieved_docs.content: Extraction of single tooth is classified as a dental procedure, indicating that it has a relationship with the broader category of dental procedures. This connection underscores that the act of extracting a single tooth is a specific type of dental intervention within the overall field of dental procedures.
